main.title: Voicebot and Chatbot Design
main.author: Rachel Batish
main.reason: Rachel Batish covers both voice and chat from a design perspective. A solid practical overview of the field that I read early in my conversation design work.

side.title: Beyond the illusion: pragmatic cues in conversation
side.author: Laura Spillner, Johanna Rockstroh, Paola Raquel Peña

wildcard.title: Studies in conversational UX design
wildcard.author: Robert J. Moore et al.
wildcard.reason: A collection of research and case studies in conversational UX from the IBM group. More rigorous than most books on the topic and useful for thinking beyond simple chatbot patterns.

all_scored.title: Voicebot and Chatbot Design
all_scored.author: Rachel Batish
all_scored.reason: Rachel Batish covers both voice and chat from a design perspective. A solid practical overview of the field that I read early in my conversation design work.

all_scored.title: Instructional story design
all_scored.author: Rance Greene
all_scored.reason: Rance Greene on using story in instructional design. Storytelling and learning design overlap a lot with what I do in conversational interfaces, so this was a natural read.

all_scored.title: Microcopy
all_scored.author: Kinneret Yifrah
all_scored.reason: More of a reference work. But remind me it's on my ereader though. Hm ereader discoverability is a thing here, don't you think?

all_scored.title: Strategic Writing for UX
all_scored.author: Torrey Podmajersky
all_scored.reason: Torrey Podmajersky on UX writing strategy. Practical and focused on the relationship between words and design decisions, which is core to my work.

all_scored.title: Voice user interface design
all_scored.author: Michael H. Cohen, James P. Giangola & Jennifer Balogh
all_scored.reason: Cohen, Giangola and Balogh wrote one of the earliest comprehensive guides to VUI design. Dated in some areas but still solid on the fundamentals.

all_scored.title: Classical Rhetoric for the Modern Student
all_scored.author: Edward P.J. Corbett
all_scored.reason: Classical rhetoric is the foundation of persuasive communication. Understanding argument and style at this level has shaped how I think about voice and tone in conversation design.

all_scored.title: Conversational UX design
all_scored.author: Robert J. Moore & Raphael Arar
all_scored.reason: Moore and Arar approach conversation design from an IBM research background. One of the more rigorous books on the topic and a regular reference.

all_scored.title: Conversations with things
all_scored.author: Diana Deibel & Rebecca Evanhoe
all_scored.reason: Deibel and Evanhoe write for conversation designers specifically. Practical, inclusive, and one of the books I'd recommend to anyone starting out in the field.

all_scored.title: Designing Connected Content
all_scored.author: Mike Atherton & Carrie Hane
all_scored.reason: Atherton and Hane on structured content and content modelling. Very relevant to how I think about knowledge architecture and reusable content.

all_scored.title: Earth Emotions
all_scored.author: Glenn A. Albrecht
all_scored.reason: I remember this being beautiful but somehow it didn't stick. This being on my ereader doesn't help

all_scored.title: How people learn
all_scored.author: Unknown
all_scored.reason: A foundational text on learning science. Relevant to anything involving instructional design, onboarding, or building products that teach.

all_scored.title: My Robot Gets Me: How Social Design Can Make New Products More Human
all_scored.author: Carla Diana
all_scored.reason: Carla Diana on social design and making products feel more human. Good for thinking about personality and emotional interaction in the interfaces I design.

all_scored.title: Robots, zombies and us
all_scored.author: Robert Kirk
all_scored.reason: Robert Kirk on machine consciousness and the philosophical problems it raises. Directly relevant to how I think about designing AI with some semblance of a coherent self.

all_scored.title: Studies in conversational UX design
all_scored.author: Robert J. Moore et al.
all_scored.reason: A collection of research and case studies in conversational UX from the IBM group. More rigorous than most books on the topic and useful for thinking beyond simple chatbot patterns.

all_scored.title: The sense of style
all_scored.author: Steven Pinker
all_scored.reason: Steven Pinker on writing well, grounded in cognitive science and linguistics. One of my go-to references for clear, direct prose.

all_scored.title: Voice content and usability
all_scored.author: Preston So
all_scored.reason: Preston So on content strategy for voice interfaces. One of the few books that seriously addresses how content needs to change when there's no screen.

all_scored.title: Beyond the illusion: pragmatic cues in conversation
all_scored.author: Laura Spillner, Johanna Rockstroh, Paola Raquel Peña

all_scored.title: Grounding in communication
all_scored.author: Herbert H. Clark, Susan E. Brennan

all_scored.title: Organising knowledge
all_scored.author: Jennifer Rowley & Richard Hartley
all_scored.reason: Rowley and Hartley on knowledge organisation: taxonomies, classification, indexing. Core stuff for anyone working with structured content or knowledge bases.

all_scored.title: Talk
all_scored.author: Elizabeth Stokoe
all_scored.reason: Elizabeth Stokoe on conversation and how talk actually works in real interactions. Her CARM approach is fascinating and has practical implications for how I design dialogue.

all_scored.title: The accidental Taxonomist
all_scored.author: Heather Hedden
all_scored.reason: Heather Hedden on taxonomy and controlled vocabularies. Very relevant to knowledge management and structured content, which feeds directly into how AI systems find and use information.

all_scored.title: The bewitching AI: the illusion of communication with LLMs
all_scored.author: Emanuele Bottazzi, Roberta Ferrario

all_scored.title: Alone together
all_scored.author: Sherry Turkle
all_scored.reason: Sherry Turkle on how technology reshapes our relationships and sense of self. Very relevant to the work I do designing conversational systems that interact with real people.

all_scored.title: Building for everyone
all_scored.author: Annie Jean-Baptiste
all_scored.reason: Annie Jean-Baptiste on inclusive product design from a Google perspective. Accessibility and inclusion are things I care about in my work.

all_scored.title: Consciousness and Robot Sentience
all_scored.author: Pentti Haikonen
all_scored.reason: Haikonen's take on machine consciousness is directly relevant to the questions I keep running into as a conversation designer: what does it mean for a machine to seem aware?

all_scored.title: Designing Across Senses
all_scored.author: Christine W. Park & John Alderman
all_scored.reason: Design that works across different senses and modalities is central to accessible conversation and voice design. This book broadened my thinking on that.

all_scored.title: Designing Voice User Interfaces
all_scored.author: Cathy Pearl
all_scored.reason: Cathy Pearl's book is a practical guide to VUI design and one of the field's classics. I come back to it regularly for reference.

all_scored.title: e-Learning and the Science of Instruction
all_scored.author: Ruth C. Clark & Richard E. Mayer
all_scored.reason: Clark and Mayer on evidence-based e-learning design. The multimedia learning principles in here are directly applicable to how I think about instructional content and conversation flows.

all_scored.title: Gamestorming
all_scored.author: Dave Gray, Sunni Brown & James Macanufo
all_scored.reason: A toolkit of workshop games for creative collaboration. Very useful when facilitating co-design sessions and ideation workshops.

all_scored.title: Inclusive Design for a Digital World
all_scored.author: Regine Gilbert
all_scored.reason: Regine Gilbert on accessibility in digital design. A good grounding in why inclusive design matters and what it actually requires in practice.

all_scored.title: It's better to be a good machine than a bad person
all_scored.author: Various
all_scored.reason: Essays on machine ethics and AI from a philosophical angle. The title alone says something useful about where the field is heading.

all_scored.title: Online invloed
all_scored.author: Bas Wouters & Joris Groen
all_scored.reason: Wouters and Groen on online persuasion and behaviour design. Useful for understanding how digital interfaces nudge people, especially relevant when designing chatbots with a commercial purpose.

all_scored.title: The Art of Co-design
all_scored.author: Jeremy Kerr et al.
all_scored.reason: NOt enough time, but I really enjoyed this so far. Might be a high contender

all_scored.title: The Design of Everyday Things
all_scored.author: Don Norman
all_scored.reason: Don Norman's foundational text on design and usability. Everything I believe about designing for human behaviour traces back somewhere to this book.

all_scored.title: Typography Beyond Borders
all_scored.author: Various
all_scored.reason: Beautifully designed, but not someting you read end-to-end. Remind me occasionally

all_scored.title: Unthought
all_scored.author: N. Katherine Hayles
all_scored.reason: On my wish list no time yet

all_scored.title: Entitled
all_scored.author: Kate Manne
all_scored.reason: Kate Manne on male entitlement and how it operates in everyday life. Important reading for anyone trying to make sense of the world we actually live in.

all_scored.title: Ambiguity in natural language
all_scored.author: Jan G. Kooij
all_scored.reason: Ambiguity is at the heart of every conversation design challenge. Kooij's linguistic take on it is rigorous and directly useful for my work.

all_scored.title: Because Internet
all_scored.author: Gretchen McCulloch
all_scored.reason: Gretchen McCulloch on how the internet has changed language. As someone who designs conversations for digital interfaces, this is genuinely useful and also just a fun read.

all_scored.title: Chatbot marketing mastery
all_scored.author: Unknown
all_scored.reason: Read it as background for chatbot-related client work. More marketing-focused than conversation design, but good for understanding the landscape.

all_scored.title: Content strategy at work
all_scored.author: Margot Bloomstein
all_scored.reason: Margot Bloomstein's take on content strategy is practical and grounded. It helped shape how I think about messaging architecture and brand voice.

all_scored.title: Conversational AI
all_scored.author: Andrew Freed
all_scored.reason: Andrew Freed's book covers the technical and design sides of conversational AI. Useful for keeping my understanding of the field current.

all_scored.title: The Definitive Guide to Conversational AI with Dialogflow
all_scored.author: Romin Irani
all_scored.reason: Romin Irani's guide to Dialogflow. Read it when working with Google's NLP tools and needed a clear technical reference.

all_scored.title: Developing enterprise chatbots
all_scored.author: Boris Galitsky
all_scored.reason: Galitsky takes a technical deep-dive into enterprise chatbots. Dense but useful when I needed to understand the NLP side of dialogue management.

all_scored.title: Discourse, consciousness and time
all_scored.author: Wallace Chafe
all_scored.reason: A dense read. Might be relevant for now though, with genAI and my garden

all_scored.title: Going Postal
all_scored.author: Terry Pratchett
all_scored.reason: Pratchett at his best. Going Postal is about the power of stories and language, which is very much my territory.
all_scored.review: Pratchett is my favorite author and brings together language, humor, deep insights, philosophy, a world view that's so deep and accurate.

all_scored.title: Hey Cyba: The inner workings of a virtual personal assistant
all_scored.author: Unknown
all_scored.reason: A look inside how a virtual personal assistant is designed and built. Practical insights from the inside of a product team, which is always more interesting than the theoretical stuff.

all_scored.title: How to talk to robots
all_scored.author: Tabitha Goldstaub
all_scored.reason: Tabitha Goldstaub on navigating the AI world, aimed at women and non-technical audiences. A useful reminder that access and legibility matter.

all_scored.title: Language acquisition
all_scored.author: Jill de Villiers & Peter de Villiers
all_scored.reason: How children acquire language is surprisingly relevant to how we design systems that process and generate language. A classic in the field, and it shows.

all_scored.title: Semiotics: The Basics
all_scored.author: Daniel Chandler
all_scored.reason: Felt like a topic I need to ready up on, but too much textbook to read for fun.

all_scored.title: Sequence Organization in Interaction
all_scored.author: Emanuel A. Schegloff
all_scored.reason: Schegloff's conversation analysis is foundational for understanding how turns and sequences actually work in dialogue. Essential reading for anyone designing conversational systems seriously.

all_scored.title: Speech and language processing
all_scored.author: Dan Jurafsky & James H. Martin
all_scored.reason: Jurafsky and Martin's textbook is a standard reference in NLP. More technical than I typically work, but essential background for understanding what's happening under the hood.

all_scored.title: Tech skills for conversation designers
all_scored.author: Hans van Dam
all_scored.reason: Hans van Dam addresses the technical skills gap in conversation design. Read it to think through what a designer actually needs to know beyond dialogue writing.

all_scored.title: The Shepherd's Crown
all_scored.author: Terry Pratchett
all_scored.reason: Pratchett's final book, and it shows. An emotional read for any fan, and a proper send-off for Tiffany Aching.
all_scored.review: See other Pratchett books. This one is extra special: It's his last.

all_scored.title: The Witch's Vacuum Cleaner
all_scored.author: Terry Pratchett
all_scored.reason: It wasn't on my radar.

all_scored.title: Thinking fast and slow
all_scored.author: Daniel Kahneman
all_scored.reason: Kahneman on how we actually make decisions. Understanding cognitive biases is useful in every aspect of design, and this is the most thorough account of them.

all_scored.title: Beyond anthropomorphism: a spectrum of interface metaphors for LLMs
all_scored.author: Jianna So, Connie Cheng, Sonia Krishna Murthy

all_scored.title: Designing the Mind
all_scored.author: Ryan Bush
all_scored.reason: Ryan Bush on deliberate self-development through applied philosophy and psychology. Picked it up out of personal curiosity about cognitive habits and growth.

all_scored.title: The Inner Game of Tennis
all_scored.author: W. Timothy Gallwey
all_scored.reason: Gallwey on the mental game of performance. Ostensibly about tennis but really about how we get in our own way. Applies well beyond sport.

all_scored.title: Thought as a substrate in human-AI interaction
all_scored.author: Xingyu Bruce Liu

all_scored.title: Toward metaphor-fluid conversation design for VUIs
all_scored.author: Smit Desai, Jessie Chin, Dakuo Wang, Benjamin Cowan, Michael Twidale

all_scored.title: Bacteria to AI
all_scored.author: N. Katherine Hayles
all_scored.reason: Reading now, March 2026

all_scored.title: A Room of One's Own
all_scored.author: Virginia Woolf
all_scored.reason: Just new

all_scored.title: Grounding gaps in language model generations
all_scored.author: Omar Shaikh, Kristina Gligorić, Ashna Khetan, Matthias Gerstgrasser, Diyi Yang, Dan Jurafsky

all_scored.title: Interacting with thoughtful AI
all_scored.author: Xingyu Bruce Liu, Haijun Xia, Xiang Anthony Chen

all_scored.title: Shaking Hands With Death
all_scored.author: Terry Pratchett
all_scored.reason: Pratchett's lecture on assisted dying. Short but powerful, and very him.
all_scored.review: Always interesting to read about my favorite author, especially on the topic of freely chosen death.

all_scored.title: I, robot
all_scored.author: Isaac Asimov
all_scored.reason: Asimov's robot stories are the starting point for thinking about AI ethics and the Three Laws. Reading it as a conversation designer gives you a completely different angle.

all_scored.title: Machines like me
all_scored.author: Ian McEwan
all_scored.reason: Ian McEwan imagines what happens when AI becomes indistinguishable from human. Reading literary fiction about AI is a good way to think through the ethics without the jargon.

all_scored.title: The Complete Robot
all_scored.author: Isaac Asimov
all_scored.reason: I ocassionally read a story, but wasn't aware that this was on my ereader. Might be a nice on the side book.

all_scored.title: 1Q84
all_scored.author: Haruki Murakami
all_scored.reason: Not on my radar; I really liked previous Murakami books

all_scored.title: A Court of Thorns and Roses
all_scored.author: Sarah J. Maas
all_scored.reason: Popular fantasy romance series that keeps appearing on reading lists. Curious what all the fuss is about.

all_scored.title: Carrie's War
all_scored.author: Nina Bawden
all_scored.reason: A British children's classic. On my list because of my interest in classic British literature and storytelling.

all_scored.title: DallerGut Dream Department
all_scored.author: Sandy Joosun Lee, Miye Lee
all_scored.reason: Korean novel about a dream shop. Charming premise and exactly the kind of gentle magical realism I enjoy between heavier reads.

all_scored.title: Het lied van ooievaar en dromedaris
all_scored.author: Anjet Daanje
all_scored.reason: Not on my radar. I feel I should read more on the topic of colonialism but it feels like a chore

all_scored.title: Hoe duur was de suiker
all_scored.author: Cynthia McLeod
all_scored.reason: should read, but theme doesn't appeal at all

all_scored.title: Inferno
all_scored.author: Dan Brown
all_scored.reason: Dan Brown is guilty pleasure reading. Fast, plotty, and set in beautiful places. Sometimes that's exactly what you need.

all_scored.title: Krekel
all_scored.author: Annet Schaap
all_scored.reason: Recent gift from a friend. Feels like a wonderful ready, but also like a waste of time considering the other books that are waiting and might be more relevant

all_scored.title: Last and First Men
all_scored.author: Olaf Stapledon
all_scored.reason: Scifi from the olden days always feels meh/masculine to me. Might be a bias

all_scored.title: Min of meer opmerkelijke gebeurtenissen...
all_scored.author: Cindy Hoetmer
all_scored.reason: Didn't like the writing style I think.It's been a while. Might be easy to finish though, for a dopamine hit.

all_scored.title: Slant
all_scored.author: Greg Bear
all_scored.reason: Not even sure whether I finished this. ONe of these 'masculine'  scifi books that I feel I should ready. Perhaps scifi is not my thing But relevant for work

all_scored.title: The Invention of Wings
all_scored.author: Sue Monk Kidd
all_scored.reason: Recent purchase

all_scored.title: Artificial Intelligence: A guide for thinking humans
all_scored.author: Melanie Mitchell
all_scored.reason: Melanie Mitchell cuts through the hype with clear-eyed analysis. I wanted a grounded perspective on AI that doesn't either panic or cheer.

all_scored.title: Hello world
all_scored.author: Hannah Fry
all_scored.reason: Hannah Fry explains algorithms and AI to a general audience in a way that's actually fair and nuanced. Exactly the kind of clear thinking about AI that I want more of.

all_scored.title: Hostage at the Table
all_scored.author: George Kohlrieser, Joe W. Forehand
all_scored.reason: Lost track, it got lost on my ereader

all_scored.title: How We Became Posthuman
all_scored.author: N. Katherine Hayles
all_scored.reason: On my wishlist, no time yet

all_scored.title: Human factors and voice interaction systems
all_scored.author: Unknown
all_scored.reason: Technical and human-factors perspective on voice systems. Useful when digging into the ergonomics and cognitive load side of voice interaction design.

all_scored.title: I'd blush if I could
all_scored.author: UNESCO
all_scored.reason: UNESCO report on gender bias in AI assistants. Directly relevant to my work: the voice and persona of conversational AI reflects and reinforces cultural assumptions about gender.

all_scored.title: Natural language processing with Python
all_scored.author: Steven Bird, Ewan Klein & Edward Loper
all_scored.reason: The standard introduction to NLP with Python. Worked through it to get a better technical grounding in how language models and text processing actually work.

all_scored.title: Natural Language Processing with Python and spaCy
all_scored.author: Yuli Vasiliev
all_scored.reason: Hands-on NLP with spaCy, the library I used most when experimenting with text analysis. A practical complement to the NLTK book.

all_scored.title: Nouveau Fuck
all_scored.author: Stella Bergsma
all_scored.reason: Hated it

all_scored.title: Rebooting AI
all_scored.author: Gary Marcus & Ernest Davis
all_scored.reason: Marcus and Davis make a sober case for what AI can and can't do, and what it would actually take to get to general intelligence. A grounding read for anyone working in the field.

all_scored.title: The Smart wife
all_scored.author: Yolande Strengers & Jenny Kennedy
all_scored.reason: Strengers and Kennedy on the gendered design of smart home AI. Essential context for anyone designing voice assistants: who gets to be the default voice, and what that communicates.

all_scored.title: Witte onschuld
all_scored.author: Gloria Wekker
all_scored.reason: should read, but topic is so not appealing.

all_scored.title: You Look Like a Thing and I Love You
all_scored.author: Janelle Shane
all_scored.reason: Janelle Shane on how AI really works, through funny and illuminating experiments. One of the most enjoyable AI books I've read, and it actually teaches you something.

all_scored.title: Battlefield Yorkshire
all_scored.author: David Cooke
all_scored.reason: History of battles fought on English soil, which ties into a personal interest in British history and walks around Yorkshire.

all_scored.title: De eilanden van goed en kwaad
all_scored.author: Adwin de Kluyver
all_scored.reason: Recent purchase

all_scored.title: Invisible Women
all_scored.author: Caroline Criado Perez
all_scored.reason: Caroline Criado Perez on the gender data gap. An important and infuriating book that changed how I think about whose needs get centred in design.

all_scored.title: Kies toch niet steeds de kant van de macht
all_scored.author: Kim van Keien, Dielwert Kuijpers
all_scored.reason: ONe of my husband's books that happened to end up on my bookshelf

all_scored.title: Reasons to Stay Alive
all_scored.author: Matt Haig
all_scored.reason: Matt Haig on depression and anxiety, written from the inside. Honest, warm, and accessible in a way that most mental health books aren't.

all_scored.title: Terry Pratchett (biography)
all_scored.author: Craig Cabell
all_scored.reason: No on my radar, wasn't aware that this was on my ereader

all_scored.title: The Big Con
all_scored.author: Mariana Mazzucato, Rosie Collington
all_scored.reason: Not sure what this is about or whether I started it.

all_scored.title: The Bowery Boys
all_scored.author: Greg Young, Tom Meyers
all_scored.reason: The Bowery Boys podcast companions in book form. New York City history is a long-running personal interest, and this covers exactly the gritty side I find most interesting.

all_scored.title: The Bowery
all_scored.author: Eric Ferrara
all_scored.reason: A focused history of the Bowery neighbourhood in New York. Picked it up out of the same interest in NYC history that led me to the Bowery Boys podcast.

all_scored.title: Wat is je overkomen?
all_scored.author: Oprah Winfrey, Bruce D. Perry
all_scored.reason: Oprah and Bruce Perry on trauma and how it shapes us. A personal read, picked up because I wanted to understand more about how early experiences affect how people communicate.

all_scored.title: Tools for thought
all_scored.author: Lev Tankelevitch, Elena L. Glassman, Jessica He, Aniket Kittur, Mina Lee, Srishti Palani, Advait Sarkar, Gonzalo Ramos, Yvonne Rogers, Hari Subramonyam
